fr:mainmatter.html:p: I am a software engineer working with theorem provers, algebraic effects, and software verification
This site was made with Forester, Search with CTRL+K.

fr:title.text: What's Next for the Heft Prover

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: What's Next for the Heft Prover

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I'm at a bit of a crossroads in the implementation of my HOL prover. Things are in quite a good state at the moment, and I can prove lots of things as is, right now the proofs are quite comparable to their counterparts in Coq. I was originally planning on spending the next chunk of work rewriting rewrite.ml, but now I'm having second thoughs. The rewriting system isn't very fast and doesn't have higher order matching, but honestly for the time being I'm not sure how much that matters. I'm not trying to build the fastest prover after all, I'm just trying to learn all I can about the application of algebraic effects to a tactic system, and though rewriting that exposed rewrite locations as choices would be interesting, I think this would be of limited use due to how severely large the space of potential rewrites are.

Instead of working on the rewriting system, I could move on to some annoyances I've had around induction and variable name capture, but again these don't really block any proofs currently, and are more of an annoyance. I would love to remove the inductive theorem generation from the trusted base and make my induction more general, but thats a long ways out and the payoff is not worth the significant investment right now.

So where does this leave us? If my goal is to really know the in's and out's of the effectful architecture, then I should rather spend my time experimenting with interesting ways of implementing different tactics, combinators, and search, and taking good notes on these experiments. Maybe a more formal description of the architecture would be more worthwhile, giving me sound semantics for the rules around correct use in the system, rules that have been implicit so far. On the other hand, maybe I'm closer to writing a paper than I realize, and maybe the existing work is already a decent contribution.
Lets go through the project from the ground up and see what we have.

Heft is a theorem prover that leverages algebraic effects to implicitely model the control flow of proof refinement. A small set of straightforward effects give a surprisingly flexable, performant, and extendable base to write tactics, search procedures, combinators (tacticals), and interactive elements. Heft integrates closely with the OCaml ecosystem, leveraging OCaml PPX for term construction and management of runtime theory dependencies.

The question is whether this work will be framed as a case study or as a more targeted technical contribution. The case for a case study would be that it would be easier to write, and that I could take advantage of both the wins and losses by documenting both. For a more techincal contribution, it would carry more weight academically, but would require some more research into proof refinement and the rules that make things sound.
A case report seems like a better fit for the amount of work and experience I have, this would probably get me good exposure while not risking overclaiming.

I think a good approach to making meaningful progress on this would be to set up an outline, and write a tree for each leave of the outline. This will at least give me an informal draft that I can start with when writing the first draft.

The implementation of a tactic based proof refinement system is a control-flow problem. There is a clear delineation within most tactics, the code that executes before requesting child subgoals, and the code afterwards, which takes the proofs of those subgoals and uses them to justify the original goal of the tactic. Normally(TODO: expand what normally means and link to provers that use this approach) this "justification" code exists in the form of a callback or thunk, which completes a proof by chaining together these justifications.

html:ul.html:li: Proof refinement as language of effects
Inspecting the runtime costs of algebraic effects in a real prover

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Overall the results show a convenient interface for writing tactics and tacticals that compose well with automation and cross cutting concerns like tracing, resource limiting, lemma/rule availablility, and search.
TODO: source of a tactic compared with the source in another assistant, what is made explicit/implicit in Heft vs others
TODO: source examples of main combinator types like control, rules, tracing, and search
The LCF architecture is a way of writing theorem provers with a proven history of implementations and a focus on maintaining trust with arbitrary scaling. Heft follows this architecture as well.
TODO
TODO

fr:title.text: Critical review of Heft with respect to this architecture

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Critical review of Heft with respect to this architecture

fr:mainmatter.html:p: TODO

Algebraic effects lie at the core of Heft's approach to proof refinement and deserve some context. Heft takes full advantage of OCaml's implementation of effects, which has some serious limitations currently, but still provides the critical features needed for the system to demonstrate how effects fit into proof refinement.
TODO
TODO
TODO

fr:title.text: Existing Approaches to Tactic Engines and Search

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Existing Approaches to Tactic Engines and Search

fr:mainmatter.html:p: There are many existing approaches to implementing theorem prover 'user space' (tactics and simple built-in automation), and I will lay out some of the prevailing approaches now.
The clean justification approach

TODO
TODO
TODO

fr:title.text: Built-in automation in modern proof assistants

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Built-in automation in modern proof assistants

fr:mainmatter.html:p: TODO

fr:title.text: The Effects and What They Mean

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The Effects and What They Mean

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Heft has a small set of effects that provide all of the extensibility needed for proof refinement. This small set of operations gives the ability for normal proof refinement, backtracking search, resource limits, tracing, and tactic metadata tracking.

fr:title.text: The essential effects for proof refinement

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The essential effects for proof refinement

fr:mainmatter.html:p: TODO
TODO
TODO
TODO
TODO
TODO
TODO
TODO

fr:title.text: Effects in Key Tactics and Combinators

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Effects in Key Tactics and Combinators

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Here we will look into how the core effects are used and composed to get working tactics that stay flexible across interpretations of the effects.
TODO (which tactics to look at; do I cover all effects or just the core ones for refinement?)

fr:title.text: Comparison with Tactic Implementation in Other Provers

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Comparison with Tactic Implementation in Other Provers

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Here we will discuss how this implementation stacks up to the major proof assistants.

fr:title.text: Strategy Control via Handlers and Combinators

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Strategy Control via Handlers and Combinators

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now we move on to some clear benefits of this refinement approach. We will look at some examples where tactics are able to run under different handlers to suit multiple use cases.
TODO
TODO

fr:title.text: Handler Stack Nesting in a Simple Proof

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Handler Stack Nesting in a Simple Proof

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Here we'll take a look at an acutal proof and essentially 'inline' the handlers at an arbitrary point in the proof to get a clear understanding of handler nesting depth, handler semantics, and how sequencing combinators work
TODO
Here we will take a look at the intuitive DFS implementation within Heft, as well as a more experimental best-first approach, and see how different proofs perform under both handlers.

Whats nice about search in Heft is that search is a handler that will automatically search the 'choose-space' of the proof. This essentially means that search is not only search over tactics, but could be a search over all the existential instantiations of an existing proof with a given handler, or say, a search of cartesian product of all the potential rule applications at multiple different parts of a proof at once.
TODO
TODO: I'll likely want to look through the literature for a canonical example. Likely we can just do list append or something, along with a proof of bound on length.

fr:title.text: Comparison of One Proof Across Search Handlers

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Comparison of One Proof Across Search Handlers

fr:mainmatter.html:p: TODO: This example should be chosen well. Likely we should have more than one proof so we can show that one only succeed under the best-first handler, but one succeeds in both cases, but with different proofs.

Overall, performance is close to HOL-light, with kernel operations being nearly the same as HOL light, and the tactics system adding a small overhead that does increase with deep searches, but under normal circumstances is very acceptable.
TODO: missing the resumption cleanup, and the implications
TODO: traces of the overall overhead of effects related operations on the prover under different types of proofs
TODO: Maybe a more nuanced analysis with respect to cache locality vs the normal monadic or justification thunk based approaches

To be clear, some bugs with search were quite hard to nail down, in large part due to the use of effects. Reasoning about multiple continuations is not something that most programmers are familiar with, so it can be a bit of a learning curve.
On the bright side, once an implementation compiled, it was usually close to correct.
TODO

As mentioned previously, the OCaml type system is not aware of effects, so ensuring effects are handled is a programmer responsibility. This isn't a huge issue and during the entire development of Heft I only had 'Effect not handled' exceptions a handful of times. Typed effects would be helpful for correctness though, as when nesting handlers and tactics, it would be good to see which effects 'leak' out, as there are rules that must be respected to maintain correct behavior of the system.
TODO

This is a great benefit to Heft's refinement approach. Most tactics take no arguments, so the behavior of all tactics is augmented in the same way, by handlers. Using these handlers with tactics reduces the human memory needed for supplying specific tactic DSL syntax, and in general I have found this approach to be quite ergonomic.
TODO: examples
TODO: some examples and compare with coq/lean. Argue for the value of introspection

fr:title.text: Choice Gives Rich Information for Best-First Queue

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Choice Gives Rich Information for Best-First Queue

fr:mainmatter.html:p: TODO: Show an over-the-top rich type of the best first implementation. A good place for machine learning as well when combined with tracing
TODO: composability, readibility, ease of writing tactics
TODO: confusing errors, loops if contract isn't respected, type signature isn't honest if you don't know about effects

fr:title.text: Tracked Effects for Sound Effect Composition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Tracked Effects for Sound Effect Composition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: TODO: this needs to happen, it would make the contracts of the system enforcable at compile time, giving us a typed tactic DSL

fr:title.text: Formalizing Effect Semantics for Proof Refinement

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Formalizing Effect Semantics for Proof Refinement

fr:mainmatter.html:p: TODO: can the language of effects make proof refinement theory easier to reason about?

I found out that ocaml continuations are not really understood by the garbage collector. This means that you have to be very careful to either resume or discontinue everything, which I was not doing. A lot of sites where I didn't resume I just ignored the continuation, but this is a severe mistake that lead to >7Gb reserved memory in some of my heavy proof search or case bashing proofs.
The trick for dealing with this would have been obvious if I read the docs:
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: exception Unwind Gc.finalise (fun k -> try ignore (discontinue k Unwind) with _ -> ()) k
fr:mainmatter.html:p: I could see some real value in this little snippet being a library function, or at least calling a bit more attention to it.
Overall my memory usage went from disgustingly linear to the point of exhaustion on my old laptop, to a mere 60mb constant with healthy minor gc collections.

fr:title.text: Limits of Parallelism in Theorem Provers

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Limits of Parallelism in Theorem Provers

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Though HOL provers don't have much interdependence between subgoals, there are limits to how much general execution of tactics can be parallelized.
For example, take the proof of this proposition.
The proof follows, as a linear application of tactics.
The final goal can be solved directly with the assumption a, finishing the proof.
Though these are three problems that aren't inherently dependent on each other, one can only know about the next problem by executing a tactic on the previous subgoal.

If we knew all of the subgoals ahead of time we could parallelize their execution 'completely', but in the case of a tactic producing a single subgoal like above this is not helpful, as most of actual code in a given tactic is used in formulating the next subproblem anyway.

What this means is that although I can't write a generic tactic engine that is parallel, I can parallelize the situations where its cheap to figure out the next n subgoals compared cost of solving those subgoals. I did this with a parallel combinator using OCaml domainslib, which works well and give near perfect speedups when the effort needed on the subgoals is evenly spread between them. This mostly helps with case-bashing proofs and situations like conj where both sides are often of similar difficulty.
I'm quite happy with the ability to switch out a @>> for a @>>! and get some parallelism though.
docs for the main module of my theorem prover

While building a proof-of-concept of fuel-based general recursion, I decided that a merge-sort implementation would be a good stopping point. Merge-sort cannot be written in a structurally recursive way, due the necessity of recursion on a symmetrical split of the list being sorted. In addition to this, the auxiliary function 'merge', used to merge two sorted lists, is also not structurally recursive. So this should be a good test of both a more complex definition and a definition which uses other non-structurally recursive functions in its definition.

While other systems generally set up machinery for proving well-foundedness of a measure function (over the arguments), I was curious if simply defining these functions with a fuel argument could get me the same results. Essentially the goal is to get to an 'unfolding lemma' which allows us to abstract away the fuel based definition by proving that sufficient fuel exists and that using this fuel ensures the function's totality and equality with the body of the fuel-based definition. More concretely for merge, this looks something like the following two proofs.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
let%expect_test "merge fuel sufficient" = let prg = {| variable fuel a0 a0': nat variable xs ys x a1 a1' x' : list nat
theorem merge_fuel_sufficient : forall λfuel. forall λxs. forall λys. imp (nat_lt (plus (length xs) (length ys)) fuel) (exists λx. (eq (merge_aux fuel xs ys) (some x))) term xs : xs term ys : ys term consa01 : (cons a0 a1) term consa01' : (cons a0' a1') term a1 : a1 term a1' : a1' term x : x term wit : (cons a0 x') term wit2 : (cons a0' x')
|} in
let xs = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "xs" in let ys = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "ys" in let wit = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "wit" in let wit2 = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "wit2" in let a1 = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "a1" in let a1' = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "a1'" in let consa01 = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "consa01" in let consa01' = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "consa01'" in let goal = ([], List.hd (Elaborator.goals_from_string prg)) in let proof = induct >> intros >> simp_asm >> false_elim >> intros >> simp >> with_arbitrary_term xs destruct >> elim_disj_asm >> simp >> with_arbitrary_term ys exists >> refl >> with_repeat elim_exists_asm >> simp >> with_arbitrary_term ys destruct >> elim_disj_asm >> simp >> with_arbitrary_term consa01 exists >> refl >> with_repeat elim_exists_asm >> simp >> cond >> simp >> with_first (with_assumptions rewrite_asm) >> with_first (with_assumptions rewrite_asm) >> with_proven [ "length_cons" ] rewrite_asm >> with_proven [ "add_suc_l" ] rewrite_asm >> with_proven [ "lt_suc_suc" ] rewrite_asm >> spec_asm a1 >> spec_asm consa01' >> mp_asm >> elim_exists_asm >> simp >> with_arbitrary_term wit exists >> refl >> simp >> with_first (with_assumptions rewrite_asm) >> with_first (with_assumptions rewrite_asm) >> with_proven [ "plus_comm" ] rewrite_asm >> with_proven [ "length_cons" ] rewrite_asm >> with_proven [ "add_suc_l" ] rewrite_asm >> with_proven [ "plus_comm" ] rewrite_asm >> with_proven [ "lt_suc_suc" ] rewrite_asm >> spec_asm consa01 >> spec_asm a1' >> mp_asm >> elim_exists_asm >> simp >> with_arbitrary_term wit2 exists >> refl in run_proof ~name:"merge_fuel_sufficient" ~notrace:true goal proof; [%expect {| ======================================== ∀x. ∀xs. ∀ys. nat_lt (plus (length xs) (length ys)) x ==> ∃x. merge_aux x xs ys = some x
Proof Complete! with fuel: 404 |}]

html:p.html:em: where (length xs + length ys) is our 'measure', we say that any fuel greater than our measure results in 'something'

fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
let%expect_test "merge unfolding lemma" = let prg = {| variable fuel : nat variable h y' a0 a0' : nat variable xs ys x t ys' a1 a1' witness : list nat
theorem merge_unfold: forall λxs. forall λys. (eq (merge xs ys) (list_match xs (ys) (λh. λt. (list_match ys (cons h t) (λy'. λys'. COND (nat_lt h y') (cons h (merge t (cons y' ys'))) (cons y' (merge (cons h t) ys'))))))) term xs : xs term ys : ys term suf : exists (λx. eq (merge_aux (suc (plus (length a1') (suc (length a1)))) a1' (cons a0 a1)) (some x)) term suf2 : exists (λx. eq (merge_aux (suc (plus (length a1') (suc (length a1)))) (cons a0' a1') a1) (some x))
|} in
let xs = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "xs" in let suf = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "suf" in let suf2 = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "suf2" in let ys = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "ys" in let goal = ([], List.hd (Elaborator.goals_from_string prg)) in let proof = intros >> with_arbitrary_term xs destruct >> with_arbitrary_term ys destruct >> with_repeat elim_disj_asm >> simp >> with_repeat elim_exists_asm >> simp >> with_repeat elim_exists_asm >> simp >> with_repeat elim_exists_asm >> with_definition [ "merge" ] rewrite >> beta >> with_first (with_definition [ "merge_aux" ] rewrite) >> with_repeat (with_first (with_assumptions rewrite)) >> simp ~exclude:[ "merge"; "merge_aux" ] >> cond >> simp ~exclude:[ "merge"; "merge_aux" ] >> with_arbitrary_term suf have >> with_proven [ "merge_fuel_sufficient" ] apply_thm >> simp >> elim_exists_asm >> with_first (with_assumptions rewrite) >> simp ~exclude:[ "merge"; "merge_aux" ] >> with_definition [ "merge" ] rewrite >> beta >> with_proven [ "length_cons" ] rewrite >> with_first (with_assumptions rewrite) >> simp >> simp ~exclude:[ "merge"; "merge_aux" ] >> with_arbitrary_term suf2 have >> with_proven [ "merge_fuel_sufficient" ] apply_thm >> simp >> with_proven [ "plus_suc" ] rewrite >> simp >> elim_exists_asm >> with_first (with_assumptions rewrite) >> simp ~exclude:[ "merge"; "merge_aux" ] >> with_definition [ "merge" ] rewrite >> beta >> with_proven [ "length_cons" ] rewrite >> with_first (with_proven [ "plus_suc" ] rewrite_asm) >> with_first (with_proven [ "plus_comm" ] rewrite) >> with_first (with_proven [ "plus_suc" ] rewrite) >> with_first (with_proven [ "plus_comm" ] rewrite) >> with_first (with_assumptions rewrite) >> simp in run_proof ~name:"merge_unfold" ~notrace:true goal proof; [%expect {| ======================================== ∀xs. ∀ys. merge xs ys = list_match xs ys (λh. λt. list_match ys (cons h t) (λy'. λys'. COND (nat_lt h y') (cons h (merge t (cons y' ys'))) (cons y' (merge (cons h t) ys'))))
Proof Complete! with fuel: 1197 |}]

html:p.html:em: where the list_match expression is exactly the body of the fuel-based definition, essentially lifting us from using merge_aux to using merge directly

fr:mainmatter.html:p: At this point we could then remove merge_aux from our simplifier and definitions, essentially never using it again and preferring the unfolding lemma instead as if it was defined this way from the start.

With the necessary definitions of merge : list nat -> list nat -> list nat, take : nat -> list nat -> list nat, and drop drop : nat -> list nat -> list nat in place, merge_sort_aux can be defined as the fuel-based stand in for our eventual merge_sort.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: def merge_sort_aux : nat -> list nat -> option (list nat) | zero => λxs. none | suc n => λxs. COND (nat_le (length xs) (suc zero)) (some xs) ((λhalf_length. option_match (merge_sort_aux n (take half_length xs)) (none) (λleft. option_match (merge_sort_aux n (drop half_length xs)) (none) (λright. some (merge left right)) ) ) (div (length xs) (suc (suc zero))))

html:p.html:em: a little ugly without let bindings or numerals, but a fairly standard definition that avoids a dedicated split function

fr:mainmatter.html:p: I should also note that I ran into some difficulty when trying to compute with merge_sort_aux on concrete data. For instance, proving merge_sort_aux 8 [3, 1, 2] = some [1, 2, 3] required a very specific ordering of rewrites to avoid explosion of unfolding branches that wouldn't end up being relevant:
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: let proof = rw_def "merge_sort_aux" >> simp ~exclude >> with_repeat @@ rw_def "div" >> with_repeat @@ rw_def "div_aux" >> simp ~exclude >> rw_def "merge_sort_aux" >> simp ~exclude >> rw_def "merge_sort_aux" >> simp ~exclude >> with_repeat @@ rw_def "div" >> with_repeat @@ rw_def "div_aux" >> simp ~exclude >> rw_def "merge_sort_aux" >> simp ~exclude >> rw_def "merge_sort_aux" >> simp ~exclude >> rw_thm "merge_unfold" >> simp ~exclude >> rw_thm "merge_unfold" >> simp ~exclude >> rw_thm "merge_unfold" >> simp ~exclude >> rw_thm "merge_unfold" >> simp ~exclude >> rw_thm "merge_unfold" >> simp ~exclude >> rw_thm "merge_unfold" >> simp ~exclude
fr:mainmatter.html:p: I believe that this is a common pattern in HOL light, where the simplifier is quite naive like mine, and there is no generic compute for these types of tests.
Now to prove sufficiency and unfolding for this definition:
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
let%expect_test "merge sort sufficient" = let prg = {| variable fuel n0 : nat variable xs x : list nat
theorem merge_sort_fuel_sufficient: forall λfuel. forall λxs. imp (nat_lt (length xs) fuel) (exists λx. (eq (merge_sort_aux fuel xs) (some x)))
term xs : xs term left : (take (div (length xs) (suc (suc zero))) xs) term right : (drop (div (length xs) (suc (suc zero))) xs)
term right_oblig : nat_lt (length (drop (div (length xs) (suc (suc zero))) xs)) n0 term left_oblig : nat_lt (length (take (div (length xs) (suc (suc zero))) xs)) n0 |} in
let xs = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "xs" in let left = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "left" in let right = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "right" in (* let left_oblig = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "left_oblig" in *) (* let right_oblig = Elaborator.term_from_string prg "right_oblig" in *) let goal = ([], List.hd (Elaborator.goals_from_string prg)) in let proof = induct >> intros >> simp_asm >> false_elim >> intros >> with_first (with_definition [ "merge_sort_aux" ] rewrite) >> beta >> cond >> with_first (with_assumptions rewrite) >> simp ~exclude:[ "merge_sort_aux"; "take"; "drop"; "div"; "merge" ] >> with_arbitrary_term xs exists >> refl >> with_first (with_assumptions rewrite) >> simp ~exclude:[ "merge_sort_aux"; "take"; "drop"; "div"; "merge" ] >> spec_asm left >> spec_asm right >> sorry in run_proof ~name:"merge_sort_fuel_sufficient" ~notrace:true goal proof; [%expect {| ======================================== ∀x. ∀xs. nat_lt (length xs) x ==> ∃x. merge_sort_aux x xs = some x
Proof Complete! with fuel: 100 |}]

html:p.html:em: I added an exclusion list to the simplifier to help out until I'm sure I can remove these auxiliary functions from the rule set. Eventually I should only need the unfolding lemma, i.e. merge_unfold but not merge or merge_aux

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This proof is in progress, but requires lemmas around take/drop that I need to backfill. Overall, I think that the fuel-based approach is fine for a smaller number of these types of generally recursive definitions, but requires a meaningfully large proof burden per definition. Long term, well-foundedness is the right move for a ergonomic prover and provides an easier, more automation-friendly obligation.

I took some time to look into the construction of number systems in a HOL environment. It seems that Harrison does something a bit peculiar in HOL-light, rather than building the number tower in a progressive way, moving from naturals -> integers -> rationals -> reals, he instead builds the real numbers as a quotient type and get the others through sub-typing.

To inform myself a bit I looked at A.H. Lightstone's "Symbolic Logic and the Real Number System" and found some interesting symmetries in the construction of integers and rationals that I wasn't aware of. Assuming we start with naturals and pairs as they're defined in Heft:
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
inductive nat := | zero : nat | suc : nat -> nat
vartype a b inductive pair := | pair : a -> b -> pair a b
fr:mainmatter.html:p: We can build integers as a subtype of pairs of naturals where the right and left represent the positive and negative components, respectively. Here it would be something like:
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: variable a b c d : nat subtype int := pair a b where (eq a zero) \/ (eq b zero)

html:p.html:em: (pseudocode as I don't have object syntax for subtypes yet)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Which gives us a canonical representation to distinguish between (5,3) and (6,4), for instance.

The symmetry I was referring to is that rationals are defined almost the same way, with a pair, this time representing the numerator and denominator. Here we use an integer numerator to keep the sign, and a natural denominator for simplicity:
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: variable a : int variable b : nat subtype rat := pair a b where (nat_lt zero b) ∧ (gcd (abs a) b = suc zero)

html:p.html:em: (pseudocode as I don't have object syntax for subtypes yet)

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Which gives us a canonical representation to distinguish between (2,3) and (6,9), for instance.
This is more obvious when looking more abstractly at their equivalence relations, with addition vs multiplication being the only difference:
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
for integers (a, b) === (c, d) when a + d = b + c
for rationals (a, b) === (c, d) when a * d = b * c
fr:mainmatter.html:p: For Heft I will elect to go the more traditional route of the number tower, as I think it will make for a more readable and easily understood formulation at the cost of having some more fiddly definitions for the algebraic laws due to reasoning about these underlying representations directly and respecting normalization at each step. Another benefit to the more traditional approach is that I don't necessarily need to add quotient typing to the system, not that it would be a large addition.

One thing that has bothered me about the state of proof assistants today is that the two main approaches of dependent type theory (DTT) and LCF (HOL) have really significant trade-offs, especially from the perspective of someone that is only beginning their journey through the space. Most of what I'll be discussing here is coming from the perspective of someone who knows how to program, and is interested in leveraging formal verification in some way.

On one hand the dominant DTT provers (Rocq, Lean) have tons of good documentation, and Lean specifically has invested heavily in making the onboarding experience as frictionless as possible. Still, these provers ask a lot of the user. To begin proving something outside of a trivial tutorial you must:
Lean is by far the best in minimizing the burden of these issues, I think largely because of the meta-programming-based tactics system and availability of quality documentation.

The other camp of widely-used proof assistants is the HOL family, including HOL-Light, HOL4, and Isabelle/HOL. These systems take a drastically different approach the core proof assistant's construction, something I might get into another time. The main difference is that these systems don't use dependent types at all, instead embedding a small simply-typed lambda calculus as the 'term language' or sometimes referred to as 'object language' to distinguish from the implementation language (often called the meta-language, hence ML). This term language is far easier to wrap your mind around compared to DTT, but creates a different problem altogether.

Aside from the cultural issues (smaller user-base, less documentation) with HOL systems that have nothing to do with the incredible implementations that have been created, I see the main issue with usability in these systems being this multiple language stratification that I mentioned above. These systems essentially trade out the technical complexity of DTT for the mental overhead of working in a system with sometimes 4 different languages all mangled together.

The most popular HOL prover, Isabelle/HOL, illustrates my point clearly. To use Isabelle you need to learn this 'shell' language, I'm not sure whats it's called, but it serves as the main interface for creating definitions, types, and setting up proofs. Parts of this shell language are actually quoted pieces of the internal HOL term language, creating a lot of friction just to define a function for example. In addition to these two, we also have a 'tactic mode' for doing proofs manually, and another language, Isar, for structuring proofs to read more like traditional proofs. Each of these 4 languages requires it's own learning process, and the fact that they're so tightly integrated means that you can't really get started without learning a bit about all of them.

I believe that a HOL system is the right call for a widely usable proof system that shields the user from a lot of theory that comes with DTT, and the only missing piece in the current systems is a reasonable usability story.

Since Heft is based on HOL-Light, I'll go over some of the friction that system has as well. HOL-Light is essentially a stripped-down HOL core, with the kernel famously consisting of only ~700 lines of readable OCaml, the only 'trusted' code in the system as far as I know. Since this is a HOL system we have the same quotation problem, where we often have a need for referencing the term language when defining things, writing proofs, etc. HOL-Light does this with some clever but clunky quoting preprocessing, allowing for quotation of HOL terms with backticks.

HOL-Light handles interactivity by assuming the user will be working on proofs and definitions directly in the OCaml top-level, which is in my experience far less pleasant to use than working in your favorite text editor with LSP-based diagnostics and proof state display.

Until now, I've been using a small domain-specific language (DSL) for writing my HOL definitions, types, and theorems. I have some infrastructure in elaborate.ml for this purpose, as well as a lexer and parser for the language. I've run into a lot of friction using this system, as I don't have quotation like HOL-Light, requiring me to manually construct terms, elaborate them, and then use the OCaml value when specializing an assumption or asserting a intermediate lemma. To get around these issues and retain a text-editor based interactive workflow, I'm exploring OCaml PPXes as my quotation mechanism, combined with leveraging OCaml's speedy compiler to refresh my proof state with dune build --watch in another window.

To build HOL terms with a PPX, I will leverage the fact that the term language's semantics are close enough to a subset of OCaml's AST that I can write my HOL definitions, types, and goals as OCaml directly. We'll see how this works in practice but I think it's a really pragmatic path forward to a potentially beginner-friendly proof assistant. The system will then trade out a lot of the traditional proof assistant onboarding process with "just know OCaml", which I think is a far more reasonable ask. The onboarding process would potentially be:

html:ol.html:li: Add to dune-project the dependencies heft heft.ppx heft.theories and dune pkg lock
Start writing definitions and goals as valid OCaml with ppx_heft as a preprocessor
Run dune build --watch in another window to see the proof state update on each save

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The end user would see something like:

There are still a few problems to solve here, like how to tell which terms should be variables vs constants for example, since PPX extensions are a purely syntactic transformation, and I don't know how acceptable it is to do things like type inference during the translation. For now I think I can get around this by just requiring all variables to have explicit annotation, matching HOL's internal representation. Also, I still need to think about other edge cases for HOL that could be difficult or confusing to represent as OCaml, subtypes for example.

fr:title.text: Using OCaml PPX for HOL Elaboration

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Using OCaml PPX for HOL Elaboration

fr:mainmatter.html:p: On my proof assistant, I've finally made the switch to a PPX-based approach to representing my HOL language, compared to the DSL I was using before. Moving to this approach allowed me to remove around 1000 lines of pretty complex elaboration logic, as well as make my definitions and theorems more easily representable, simply as writing OCaml with some restrictions. The new syntax looks like this.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
(* Defining types*) [%%inductive type nat = Zero | Suc of nat]
(* Non-recursive definitions *) let%def pred (n : nat) : nat = match n with Zero -> Zero | Suc m -> m
(* Structurally recursive definitions *) let%primrec plus (n : nat) (m : nat) : nat = match n with Zero -> m | Suc n' -> Suc (plus n' m)
(* Proofs *) let goal = make_goal [%term forall (fun (xs : 'a list) -> length xs = Zero ==> (xs = Nil))] in run_proof goal begin induct >> intros >> refl >> intros >> simp_asm >> sym_asm >> with_first (with_rules NatTheory.nat_def.distinct rewrite_asm) >> false_elim end;
fr:mainmatter.html:p: The PPX takes a restricted set of syntactically valid OCaml code and transforms it into the equivalent HOL terms, with the addition of calling the inductive and function definition machinery at runtime.
This cut down a lot of noise in Heft, and I hope it makes the system more approachable, at least to those comfortable with OCaml.
I still have plans for some dedicated proof syntax and for subtype definitions. Something like:
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: let%thm length_zero_imp_nil (xs : 'a list) = length xs = Zero ==> (xs = Nil), begin induct >> intros >> refl >> intros >> simp_asm >> sym_asm >> with_first (with_rules NatTheory.nat_def.distinct rewrite_asm) >> false_elim end [@simp] [@quiet]
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Or maybe:
fr:mainmatter.html:pre: let%thm length_zero_imp_nil (xs : 'a list) = length xs = Zero ==> (xs = Nil) and proof = begin induct >> intros >> refl >> intros >> simp_asm >> sym_asm >> with_first (with_rules NatTheory.nat_def.distinct rewrite_asm) >> false_elim end [@simp] [@quiet]
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Where the [@simp] annotations are used in the run_proof call.

I'm looking at adding some more tactics to improve the ergonomics of my prover. I've added a discriminate tactic for goals with distinct constructor equality (Suc n = Zero), but I want to improve my contradiction reasoning a bit. Right now I have a contradict_asm which can prove things like {\neg P \vdash \bot } using a subgoal of \vdash P, but Rocq's contradict tactic is much stronger and handles 3 more distinct cases in addition to the one that my tactic does.
From Rocq's documentation:
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
A tactic for proof by contradiction. With contradict H,
1 H:~A |- B gives |- A 2 H:~A |- ~B gives H: B |- A 3 H: A |- B gives |- ~A 4 H: A |- ~B gives H: B |- ~A
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Translating this to Heft, I can have exfalso as have F >> [subgoal] >> false_elim

html:ol.html:li: Case one is just exfalso >> contradict_asm
Case two is neg_intro to get {\neg A, B \vdash \bot }, then contradict_asm to get B \vdash A
Case three is just have \neg A
Case four is neg_intro to get {A, B \vdash \bot }, then have \neg A >> [subgoal] >> neg_elim

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Essentially if the goal is a negation we call neg_intro to get it in an assumption, say A, then we take a chosen assumption (other than A), say B. If it's a negation, we contradict_asm, otherwise we just assert its negation (\neg B).
This will help in a variety of cases that are currently pretty tedious with the current negation/false related tactics.

I use neovim for development, and after the 0.12 update I was able to clean up my config quite a bit. For my own sanity and for any others that want a clean, minimalist config that includes nearly everything needed to be productive during development I've annotated the config below.
fr:mainmatter.html:pre:
-- Requires: neovim 0.12+, ripgrep (for telescope live_grep), a nerd font (optional)
-- Basic config vim.opt.tabstop = 4 vim.opt.shiftwidth = 4 vim.opt.ignorecase = true vim.opt.smartcase = true vim.opt.autoindent = true vim.opt.number = true vim.opt.clipboard = 'unnamedplus' vim.g.mapleader = " " vim.g.maplocalleader = ' ' vim.opt.ruler = true vim.opt.cursorline = true vim.opt.expandtab = true vim.opt.scrolloff = 15 vim.opt.relativenumber = true vim.cmd('dig TS 8866') -- digraph support for ⊢ vim.opt.laststatus = 2 -- Or 3 for global statusline vim.opt.statusline = " %f %m %= %l:%c of %L ♥ "
-- Plugins local function gh(repo) return 'https://github.com/' .. repo end local function cb(repo) return 'https://codeberg.org/' .. repo end
vim.pack.add({ gh('nvim-lua/plenary.nvim'), -- pretty generic dependency for other plugins gh('hrsh7th/nvim-cmp'), -- completion gh('hrsh7th/cmp-nvim-lsp'), -- more completion gh('nvim-treesitter/nvim-treesitter'), -- supports better syntax highlighting gh('nvim-telescope/telescope.nvim'), -- a fuzzy gh('nvim-telescope/telescope-frecency.nvim'), -- a helpful addition for finding files based on both how often and how recently you've opened them gh('p00f/alabaster.nvim'), -- theme cb('andyg/leap.nvim'), -- for jumping to any word on screen by prefix gh('tpope/vim-surround'), -- for wrapping parens, etc gh('tpope/vim-fugitive'), -- simple and clean interface to git for reviewing and modifying diffs gh('ellisonleao/gruvbox.nvim'), -- theme gh('olimorris/onedarkpro.nvim'), -- theme gh('ntk148v/komau.vim'), -- theme gh('airblade/vim-gitgutter'), -- show git changes on the side of the buffer gh('neovim/nvim-lspconfig'), -- lsp is now built in, but this brings all the configs for different langs as well gh('Julian/lean.nvim'), -- lean4 support gh('whonore/Coqtail'), -- rocq support gh('tomtomjhj/vsrocq.nvim'), -- rocq support gh('stevearc/oil.nvim') -- file explorer }) vim.api.nvim_create_user_command("PackUpdate", function() require("vim.pack").update() end, { desc = "Update all plugins using vim.pack" })
-- Colorscheme vim.api.nvim_create_user_command("ToggleBackground", function() if vim.o.background == 'dark' then vim.o.background = 'light' else vim.o.background = 'dark' end end, { desc = "Toggles the vim.opt.background setting" }) vim.opt.background = 'light' vim.cmd("colorscheme komau")
-- Configure plugins require('oil').setup() require('telescope').load_extension('frecency') require('telescope').setup({ defaults = { path_display = { "smart" } } }) require('lean').setup({ mappings = true, })
-- Completion setup (tab to cycle completions, enter to accept them) local cmp = require('cmp') cmp.setup({ sources = { { name = 'nvim_lsp' }, }, mapping = { ['<Tab>'] = cmp.mapping.select_next_item(), ['<S-Tab>'] = cmp.mapping.select_prev_item(), ['<CR>'] = cmp.mapping.confirm({ select = true }), ['<C-Space>'] = cmp.mapping.complete(), }, }) -- autoformat on save local format_on_save = false vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd("BufWritePre", { group = vim.api.nvim_create_augroup("FormatOnSave", { clear = true }), callback = function() if format_on_save then vim.lsp.buf.format() end end, })
vim.api.nvim_create_user_command("ToggleFormatOnSave", function() format_on_save = not format_on_save vim.notify("Format on save: " .. (format_on_save and "enabled" or "disabled")) end, { desc = "Toggles format on save" }) vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>ss', ':ToggleFormatOnSave<CR>')
-- Quickfix vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd("FileType", { pattern = "qf", callback = function() vim.keymap.set("n", "dd", function() local qflist = vim.fn.getqflist() local line = vim.fn.line(".") - 1 -- 0-indexed table.remove(qflist, line + 1) vim.fn.setqflist(qflist) end, { buffer = true }) end, })
-- Main keybindings
-- Fuzzy local builtin = require('telescope.builtin') vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>ff', builtin.find_files) -- SPACE f f finds files in the current directory, minus those in gitignore vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>fg', builtin.live_grep) -- SPACE f g finds text inside files in the current directory, minus those in gitignore vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>fb', builtin.buffers) -- SPACE f b finds files currently opened vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>fH', builtin.help_tags) -- SPACE f H looks through the help pages for neovim and plugins vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>fh', ':Telescope frecency<CR>') -- SPACE f h looks through files sorted by how often and how recently they've been opened vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>fc', builtin.git_commits) -- SPACE f c looks through git commits and shows the diffs vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>fs', builtin.git_status) -- SPACE f s looks through changed files (from git status)
-- Git vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>gb', ':Git blame<CR>') -- SPACE g b shows an inline git blame for the current file vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>gs', ':Git status<CR>') -- SPACE g s shows a quick overview of the git status vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>gg', ':Git<CR>') -- SPACE g g opens a full git fugitive window for managing changes and more vim.keymap.set('n', ']g', ':GitGutterNextHunk<CR>') -- ] g goes to the next changed piece of code vim.keymap.set('n', '[g', ':GitGutterPrevHunk<CR>') -- [ g goes to the previous changed piece of code
-- vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>b', ':ToggleBackground<CR>') -- [ g goes to the previous changed piece of code
-- Leap vim.keymap.set({ 'n', 'x', 'o' }, 's', '<Plug>(leap)') -- s triggers vim leap, to jump to another part of the screen by character tags vim.keymap.set('n', 'S', '<Plug>(leap-from-window)') -- S triggers the same but more globally
-- File management vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>o', ':Oil<CR>') -- SPACE o opens interactive file explorer with ability to rename/delete/move files as a buffer
-- Treesitter vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd("FileType", { callback = function(ev) pcall(vim.treesitter.start, ev.buf) end })
-- Specialized languages require('rocq') require('koka')
-- Normal LSP's vim.lsp.enable('lua_ls') vim.lsp.enable('ocamllsp') -- on_init = function(client) -- client.server_capabilities.semanticTokensProvider = nil -- end, vim.lsp.enable('koka') -- configured separately above vim.lsp.enable('clangd') vim.lsp.enable('millet') -- SML language server vim.lsp.enable('tinymist') -- typst language server
-- Custom macros for dumb shit
vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>cc', 'olet goal = make_goal [%term ] in')

fr:mainmatter.html:p: To best explain what a proof assistant is (not why its valuable), I will start with basic definitions and try to break it down into simple terms.
Ultimately, a proof assistant is a computer program that helps humans prove theorems.
A proposition that is true inside of a given formal system.
"either its raining outside, or its not raining outside"
A statement written in a formal language that can be true or false.
e.g. "its raining outside"
Note that this phrase doesn't have a truth value yet. It's truth is determined by the definitions and rules of the formal system that it is described within.

A formal language, combined with rules that dictate what sorts of propositions are true and false within its context. Usually a formal system is designed in such a way that False is not a theorem. Formal systems usually include axioms as well.
A formal system could be the system of simple arithmetic, say \set {0, 1, +}, along with some rules about how plus works, say \forall n. n + 0 = n (any number plus zero is that number).
An axiom is something that you believe is true without a proof.
\forall n. n = n (for any number, that number is equal to itself).

A set of symbols and words for very precisely describing something. This can be something like arithmetic (+, -, /), or propositional calculus (\land , \lor , \to ). Where in written language, the same words can mean different things based on the words and punctuation around them, in a formal language the same symbols/words mean the same thing every time they are used.

e.g. A language of simple arithmetic, where we have variables, a, b, c, a binary operator +, and parenthesis to group expressions. This would allow me to write things like (a + b) + c, but not a + (+ b)

To prove something in a formal system, you use the system's rules and axioms, along with other theorems you have already proved, to simplify the thing you're trying to prove to the point where it is obviously true by way of one of the simple rules and axioms in your system.
An example of a proof in the formal system of a basic arithmetic would be as follows.
Our arithmetic system has only three constructs:

html:ul.html:li: A function S(n) which means "the number after n".
Equality (=) meaning that two numbers are the same.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We can write numerals like 1, 2, 3 by repeated using the "next number" operator. e.g. 3 would be S(S(S 0))
We can define + using two rules:
The goal is to prove 1 + 1 = 2
To begin, lets simplify these numbers to their exact form, meaning the goal is to prove that S(0) + S(0) = S(S(0))

html:ol.html:li: First, we apply our second rule of addition to obtain S(S(0) + 0) = S(S(0)).
Then, we apply our first rule of addition to obtain S(S(0)) = S(S(0)), noticing that the inner expression is added to 0.
Finally, we can establish that this is true, because we have an equality where both sides are the same number.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Though a simple example, this already shows some of the challenges with building proofs. One needs to notice patterns in their goal, know which definitions are relevant, and provide enough detail to move between steps in the proof.

A proof assistant, sometimes called a "theorem prover" or just "prover" is distinguishable from other tools in the field by its level of interactivity. An automated theorem prover is at one end of the spectrum, where if a theorem is provable by the system then it will happen without user intervention. On the other side, there are interactive provers where each minute step must be described to the computer by a human, and the machine essentially just "checks" if the proof is valid. Many of the most interesting systems (Rocq, Lean, Isabelle/HOL) lie in between, and have strong automatic systems to help people the most boring and mechanical parts of a proof, while retiaining the ability to precisely spell out the difficult parts.
Proof refinement is a term describing the act of "breaking down" a proof into smaller more obvious sub-problems. You can think of this as problem decomposition applied to proof.
This is the primary model of proving in interactive theorem provers, generally by using tactics to break a single "goal" down into smaller/simpler "subgoals".
